agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A courier-tracking feature is being scoped where customers would see their courier's real-time location during delivery. Two internal stakeholder teams have submitted directly contradictory requirements: Customer Success demands full GPS transparency as a competitive differentiator, while Operations demands no location sharing due to driver retention concerns. This is a fundamental business conflict that cannot be resolved by engineering — it requires executive arbitration.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking / Courier Visibility","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Courier","definition":"The driver or delivery person physically transporting a package to the customer."},{"term":"Real-time location","definition":"GPS coordinates of the courier's device, updated at frequent intervals during an active delivery."},{"term":"Delivery window","definition":"The period from when a courier is dispatched for a delivery until the delivery is completed or failed."},{"term":"Location granularity","definition":"The precision level at which a courier's position is exposed — ranging from exact GPS coordinates to zone-level approximation (e.g., '2 stops away', 'nearby')."},{"term":"Driver retention","definition":"The ability to keep couriers on the platform; a key operational health metric directly tied to capacity and service levels."},{"term":"Delivery transparency","definition":"The degree to which customers have visibility into the status and progress of their delivery."}]},"business_rules":[],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Courier is stationary for extended period (e.g., traffic, break) and exact GPS is shown","expected_behavior":"Customer may perceive courier as idle or lost, generating support tickets. Courier feels monitored during legitimate breaks. This edge case illustrates why granularity matters."},{"scenario":"Courier is at a sensitive personal location (home, medical facility) at start/end of shift","expected_behavior":"Exact GPS sharing could expose private information about the courier's life outside deliveries. Any solution must scope visibility strictly to active delivery windows."},{"scenario":"Courier device GPS is inaccurate or stale","expected_behavior":"Showing 'exact coordinates' that are wrong is worse than showing an approximation. Precision claims must match actual data quality."},{"scenario":"Customer uses location data to intercept or confront courier","expected_behavior":"Safety risk to courier. This is a real-world concern that Operations is likely factoring into their position."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"If drivers leave due to surveillance concerns, the company loses delivery capacity — which directly limits revenue. Replacing drivers is expensive (recruiting, onboarding, ramp-up). Conversely, if customers cannot track deliveries, customer acquisition and retention suffer in a market where tracking is table stakes.","operational":"Driver shortage from attrition creates cascading operational failures: longer delivery times, missed SLAs, overloaded remaining drivers, further attrition. This is a death spiral. Operations is flagging a real and urgent problem.","user":"Customers expect some form of delivery tracking — this is industry standard. However, 'exact GPS coordinates at all times' is not what most customers actually need. They need confidence their package is coming and a reliable ETA. The customer need and the Customer Success team's proposed solution are not the same thing.","risk":"HIGH. Shipping either team's requirement as stated will cause material business harm. Showing exact GPS risks driver attrition and potential safety/privacy liability. Showing nothing risks customer dissatisfaction and competitive disadvantage. There may also be privacy regulation implications (GDPR, local labor laws) around sharing worker location data with third parties.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Couriers/Drivers","impact":"CRITICAL. Their physical safety, privacy, and sense of autonomy are directly at stake. They are already leaving over this issue. Their voice must be weighted heavily — without drivers, there is no business."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"HIGH. They want delivery confidence and predictability. The actual need is reliable ETAs and progress indication, not necessarily raw GPS coordinates."},{"group":"Customer Success team","impact":"MEDIUM. Their KPIs around customer satisfaction and NPS are driving their position, but their proposed solution (exact GPS) may overshoot what customers actually need."},{"group":"Operations team","impact":"CRITICAL. They are accountable for driver supply and delivery capacity. Driver attrition is an existential operational risk."},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"UNKNOWN. Sharing employee/contractor GPS data with customers may have regulatory implications depending on jurisdiction. This stakeholder has not been consulted."},{"group":"Executive leadership","impact":"HIGH. This is a strategic tradeoff between customer experience and operational capacity that requires executive-level decision-making."}]},"confidence":{"level":35,"reasoning":"Confidence is very low because the specification contains a direct, irreconcilable contradiction between two stakeholder teams, both claiming their requirement is non-negotiable. No acceptance criteria can be defined until this conflict is resolved at a level above both teams. Additionally, Legal/Compliance has not weighed in, and there is no data on what customers actually need (vs what Customer Success assumes they want).","high_confidence_areas":["This is a genuine business conflict, not a technical one","Driver retention is a real operational risk that cannot be dismissed","Some form of delivery tracking is industry-standard and expected by customers","Exact GPS coordinates are not the only way to provide delivery transparency"],"low_confidence_areas":["What level of location granularity satisfies customers without harming driver retention","Whether there are regulatory constraints on sharing courier location data","What the actual driver attrition numbers are and how directly they correlate to tracking","Whether drivers would accept a compromise (e.g., zone-level tracking) or reject any visibility"],"assumptions":["Both teams are acting in good faith and representing real concerns","Driver attrition attributed to location tracking is based on actual driver feedback, not speculation","No executive decision or company policy has already been made on this topic"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"Customer Success requires showing exact courier GPS coordinates to customers as non-negotiable. Operations requires never showing courier location to customers as non-negotiable. These requirements are mutually exclusive and cannot both be implemented. This is not a technical problem — it is a business strategy conflict that must be resolved by executive leadership with input from Legal, HR, and ideally the couriers themselves.","affected_stakeholders":["Couriers/Drivers","Customers","Customer Success","Operations","Legal/Compliance","Executive Leadership"],"options":["(A) Side with Customer Success: show exact GPS. Risk: accelerated driver attrition, potential legal exposure, safety concerns.","(B) Side with Operations: show no location. Risk: customer dissatisfaction, competitive disadvantage in a market where tracking is standard.","(C) Executive-brokered compromise: show approximate/zone-level location (e.g., 'Your courier is 3 stops away', 'Courier is nearby', map with ~500m radius blur) rather than exact GPS. This is the industry-standard approach used by most successful platforms (Uber, DoorDash, Amazon). Preserves customer confidence while protecting driver privacy.","(D) Driver-consent model: let couriers opt in/out of location sharing, with incentives for opting in. Respects driver autonomy but creates inconsistent customer experience.","(E) Commission a joint discovery: survey customers on what tracking information they actually need, and survey drivers on what level of sharing they would accept. Make a data-driven decision."],"recommendation":"Recommend Option (E) first to gather data, with Option (C) as the interim implementation. Zone-level approximate tracking is proven across the industry to satisfy customer needs without creating the surveillance dynamic that drives attrition. Exact GPS is almost certainly overshooting customer needs — customers want delivery confidence, not a coordinate pair. This decision MUST be escalated to executive leadership; engineering should not ship either team's requirement as stated."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Cannot accept Customer Success requirement as stated","business_rule_broken":"Showing exact GPS coordinates at all times directly contradicts Operations' reported driver retention crisis. Implementing this without resolving the conflict would cause measurable business harm (driver attrition) while likely overshooting actual customer needs."},{"violation":"Cannot accept Operations requirement as stated","business_rule_broken":"Showing zero location information ignores industry-standard customer expectations for delivery tracking. This would create competitive disadvantage and likely increase support ticket volume ('where is my package?') which itself is an operational burden."},{"violation":"No legal/compliance review has been conducted","business_rule_broken":"Sharing worker GPS data with third-party customers has potential privacy regulation implications (GDPR Article 6, local labor surveillance laws). No acceptance criteria should be finalized until Legal reviews the proposed approach."}]}
